revision.text:
[[commons:Category:Ami Jinja]][[ja:阿彌神社 (阿見町竹来)]] {{Expand Japanese|阿彌神社 (阿見町竹来)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Ami Shrine | native_name = 阿祢神社 | image = The front shrine of Ami shrine (Takaku, Ami town, Ibaraki prefecture).jpg | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Ami Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|36.034328|140.234726}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = {{ill|Takemikazuchi|qid=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|de|Takemikazuchi|en|Takemikazuchi|eo|Takemikazuĉi|es|Takemikazuchi|fa|تاکه‌میکازوچی|fr|Takemikazuchi|id|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ|pl|Takemikazuchi|pt|Takemikadzuchi|ru|Такэмикадзути|zh|建御雷|qid=Q1502690|1=Takemikazuchi}} }} {{nihongo|'''Ami Shrine'''|阿祢神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Hitachi Province]]. == instance of (P31) == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická svatyně|da|Shinto-tempel|de|Shintō-Schrein|el|Σιντοϊστικό Ιερό|en|Shinto shrine|eo|Ŝintoa sanktejo|es|Jinja|eu|Jinja|fa|معبد شینتویی|fi|Šintolainen pyhäkkö|fr|Sanctuaire shinto|hi|शिंतो मंदिर|hr|Šintoistički hram|hu|Sintó szentély|id|Jinja|it|Jinja|ja|神社|ka|ძინძია|ko|신사 (신토)|lb|Shintō-Schräin|mr|शिंतो मंदिर|nl|Jinja (heiligdom)|no|Jinja|or|ଜିଞ୍ଜା|pl|Jinja|pt|Templo xintoísta|ru|Дзиндзя|sc|Jinja|simple|Shinto shrine|sl|Šintoistično svetišče|sr|Шинтоистички храм|sv|Jinja|th|ศาลเจ้าชินโต|tr|Şinto tapınağı|uk|Святилище (синто)|vi|Thần xã|wuu|神社|zh_yue|神社|zh|神社|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} * {{ill|Q134917286|ltq=Q134917286|qid=Q134917286|1=Shikinaisha}} * {{ill|Q134917287|ltq=Q134917287|qid=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} * {{ill|Shikinai Ronsha|lt=Shikinai Ronsha|ja|論社|qid=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} * {{ill|Q135160342|ltq=Q135160342|qid=Q135160342|1=Kokuhei-sha}} == part of (P361) == * {{ill|Q11481683|ltq=Q11481683|qid=Q11481683|1=List of Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province}} ** Series ordinal: 5 ** P155: {{ill|Q17211507|ltq=Q17211507|qid=Q17211507|1=Tatenui Shrine (Miho, Ibaraki)}} ** P156: {{ill|Nagahatabe Shrine|lt=Nagahatabe Shrine|ja|長幡部神社 (常陸太田市)|qid=Q17227113|1=Nagahatabe Shrine (Hitachiota)}} == located in the administrative territorial entity (P131) == * {{ill|Q1203814|ltq=Q1203814|qid=Q1203814|1=Ami, Ibaraki}} * {{ill|Q1055353|ltq=Q1055353|qid=Q1055353|1=Hitachi Province}} * {{ill|Shida district|lt=Shida district|ja|信太郡|pl|Powiat Shida|qid=Q11386034|1=Shida district}} == coordinate location (P625) == * {{coord|36.034328|140.234726}} == image (P18) == * The front shrine of Ami shrine (Takaku, Ami town, Ibaraki prefecture).jpg == dedicated to (P825) == * {{ill|Takemikazuchi|qid=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|de|Takemikazuchi|en|Takemikazuchi|eo|Takemikazuĉi|es|Takemikazuchi|fa|تاکه‌میکازوچی|fr|Takemikazuchi|id|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ|pl|Takemikazuchi|pt|Takemikadzuchi|ru|Такэмикадзути|zh|建御雷|qid=Q1502690|1=Takemikazuchi}} == Kokugakuin University Digital Museum entry ID (P13677) == * 181758 ** P3831: {{ill|Q135159299|ltq=Q135159299|qid=Q135159299|1=Kokugakuin University Shrine database}} == modern shrine ranking (P13723) == * {{ill|Q90139226|ltq=Q90139226|qid=Q90139226|1=Ken-sha}} ** End time: {'time': '+1946-02-02T00:00:00Z', 'timezone': 0, 'before': 0, 'after': 0, 'precision': 11, 'calendarmodel': 'http://www.wikidata.org/entity/Q1985727'} ** P1534: {{ill|Shinto Directive|lt=Shinto Directive|en|Shinto Directive|fa|بخشنامه شینتو|fr|Directive shinto|id|Pengarahan Shinto|ja|神道指令|zh|神道指令|qid=Q3029647|1=Shinto Directive}} == legal form (P1454) == * {{ill|Q7311343|ltq=Q7311343|qid=Q7311343|1=religious corporation}} == country (P17) == * {{ill|Q17|ltq=Q17|qid=Q17|1=Japan}} == postal code (P281) == * 300-0305https://www.houjin-bangou.nta.go.jp/download/zenken/index.html == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 4050005003147https://www.houjin-bangou.nta.go.jp/download/zenken/index.html == Commons category (P373) == * Ami Jinja == said to be the same as (P460) == * {{ill|Amino Shrine|lt=Amino Shrine|qid=Q135039760|1=Amino Shrine}} ** P2868: {{ill|Q135022904|ltq=Q135022904|qid=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '茨城県稲敷郡阿見町竹来1366番地', 'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '茨城県稲敷郡阿見町竹来1366', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Ami Jinja}} [[JA:阿彌神社 (阿見町竹来)]] {{wikidata link|Q17191825}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Ami Shrine |Image = [[File:The front shrine of Ami shrine (Takaku, Ami town, Ibaraki prefecture).jpg|270px]]
Worship hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|qid=Q83273|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Inashiki District|Q1154673|qid=Q1154673|1=Inashiki District, Ibaraki}}{{ill|lt=Ami Town|Q1203814|qid=Q1203814|1=Ami, Ibaraki}} Takaku 1366 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|qid=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|qid=Q1502690|lt=Take-Mikazuchi-no-Otoko-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}} |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} (minor)
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Founded = Year 15 of {{ill|lt=Empress Suiko|Q298057|qid=Q298057|1=Empress Suiko}} ({{ill|lt=607|Q31823|qid=Q31823|1=607}}) |Main sanctuary = Three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Alternate names = Ninomiya Myōjin
Takaku Three Shrines |Pilgrimage sites = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=October 5|Q2930|qid=Q2930|1=October 5}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Ibaraki|label=Ami Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Ami Shrine''' (Ami Jinja, also written 阿弥神社) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located at Takaku 1366, {{ill|lt=Ami Town|Q1203814|qid=Q1203814|1=Ami, Ibaraki}}, {{ill|lt=Inashiki District|Q1154673|qid=Q1154673|1=Inashiki District, Ibaraki}}, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|qid=Q83273|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}} (formerly Takaku Village, {{ill|lt=Shida District|Q11386034|qid=Q11386034|1=Shida district}}). Until the early Meiji period, it was known as “Ninomiya Myōjin (Second Shrine Deity)” as the {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|qid=Q1656379|lt=Second Shrine|1=Ichinomiya}} of the former {{ill|lt=Shida District|Q11386034|qid=Q11386034|1=Shida district}}. Together with two deities enshrined in subsidiary sanctuaries, and according to another theory together with Murozaki Shrine (Ōmuro, Ami Town) and Toake Shrine (Hasamado, Ami Town), it was also called the “Takaku Three Shrines.” Along with the shrine of the same name located in Nakagō, Ami Town ({{ill|lt=Ami Shrine (Nakagō, Ami Town)|Q17191824|qid=Q17191824|lt=Ami Shrine|1=Ami Shrine (Nakagō)}}), it is one of the two shrines (minor shrines) of Shida District, {{ill|lt=Hitachi Province|Q1055353|qid=Q1055353|1=Hitachi Province}}, listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “{{ill|lt=Ami Shrine|Q16276818|qid=Q16276818|1=Ami Shrine}},” and is regarded as a {{ill|lt=candidate shrine|Q135022904|qid=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}}). Under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, it was ranked as a former prefectural shrine. [[File:Torii of Ami Shrine (Takaku, Ami town, Ibaraki prefecture).jpg|thumb|240px|{{center|Torii}}]] === Enshrined deities === [[File:Shrine within the Precinct of Ami Shrine (Takaku, Ami town, Ibaraki prefecture).jpg|thumb|160px|{{center|Subsidiary shrine}}]] ;Main deity :{{ill|qid=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|Q1502690|qid=Q1502690|lt=Take-Mikazuchi-no-Otoko-no-Mikoto|1=Takemikazuchi}} ;Associated deities :{{ill|qid=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi|UNKNOWN|qid=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi-no-Mikoto|1=Futsunushi}}, {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Koyane|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} The {{ill|lt=shintai|Q327532|qid=Q327532|1=Shintai}} (sacred object) is a round mirror. Based on traditions derived from the Futsu myth, there is also a theory that the main enshrined deity is Futsu Ōkami. === Subsidiary shrines === According to the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji Shrine Records|1=UNKNOWN}}, the following subsidiary shrines are listed: *{{ill|lt=Ōsugi Shrine|Q11435950|qid=Q11435950|1=Osugi Shrine}} ({{ill|UNKNOWN|qid=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōmononushi}}) *Musubi Shrine ({{ill|qid=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi|Q125400|qid=Q125400|lt=Taka-mi-Musubi-no-Kami|1=Takamimusubi}}) – listed as “Kōmusubi Shrine” in the Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Photo Register *Ōarai Sakatsura Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|1=Ōkuninushi|qid=Q276944}}, {{ill|lt=Sukuna-bikona|Q3275687|qid=Q3275687|lt=Sukuna-bikona-no-Mikoto|1=Sukunabikona}}) *Uba Shrine ({{ill|lt=Tamayori-hime|Q56344982|qid=Q56344982|lt=Tamayori-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Tamayori-hime}}) *Hie Shrine ({{ill|qid=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamakui-no-Kami|Q10935105|qid=Q10935105|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}) *Inari Shrine ({{ill|qid=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|qid=Q3080728|qid=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|1=Ukanomitama}}) *Ōkuninushi Shrine (Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto) *Saigami Shrine ({{ill|lt=Kunado-no-Kami|Q2963653|qid=Q2963653|lt=Kunado-no-Kami|1=Kunado-no-Kami}}, Yamata-biko-no-Kami, Yamata-bime-no-Kami) *Homusubi Shrine ({{ill|lt=Homusubi|UNKNOWN|lt=Ho-musubi-no-Kami}}) *Kotohira Shrine (Ōmononushi-no-Kami) *Ukemochi Shrine ({{ill|lt=Ukemochi-no-Kami|Q2280323|qid=Q2280323|1=Ukemochi}}) *Chikatsu Shrine ({{ill|lt=Tajikarao|UNKNOWN|lt=Tajikarao-no-Kami}}) *Tsukuyomi Shrine ({{ill|qid=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|qid=Q595520|qid=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|1=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}) *Sarutahiko Shrine ({{ill|lt=Sarutahiko|Q3090037|qid=Q3090037|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Kami|1=Sarutahiko Ōkami}}) In addition, the Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Photo Register lists one more shrine: *Ancestral Spirit Shrine within the precincts (details unknown) A distinctive feature of the precinct is that seven small paths branch off only toward the northwest from the approach, each enshrining a stone shrine or stone monument. As for wooden structures, in addition to the main sanctuary and worship hall, there is a {{ill|lt=Kagura hall|Q94498|qid=Q94498|1=Kagura-den}} built in {{ill|lt=Hōreki|Q1194397|qid=Q1194397|1=Hōreki}} 6 (1756) on the west side of the precinct, and a one-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} shrine building on the east side. According to the “Newly Compiled Gazetteer of Scenic Sites,” which states, “The main hall, worship hall, offering hall, Ōsugi hall, kagura hall, and others stand in orderly rows, displaying remarkable splendor,” this shrine building is thought to be the Ōsugi Shrine (Ōsugi Hall). Note that the shrine building of the former Kasumigaura Shrine ({{ill|lt=shinmei-zukuri|Q930545|qid=Q930545|1=Shinmei-zukuri}}) is preserved not here but at the site of Nakagō Ami Shrine. === Festivals === Until the Edo period, the festival period was from September 3 to 9 (Newly Compiled Gazetteer of Hitachi Province). According to prewar records, official and private festivals were held on September 5 ({{ill|lt=Meiji Shrine Records for Prefectural and Local Shrines|Q11486103|qid=Q11486103|1=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō}}) or October 5 (Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Photo Register), and rituals such as the Opening of the Shrine Doors Festival on January 7, the Flower Pacification Festival in April, and the Rice-Planting Festival in May were also conducted (Newly Compiled Gazetteer of Scenic Sites). The current festivals are as follows: *January 7 – Shrine Door Opening Festival *February 3 – {{ill|lt=Setsubun|Q746798|qid=Q746798|1=Setsubun}} Festival *February 20 – Spring Festival (Prayer Festival) *October 5 – {{ill|lt=Main annual festival|Q11385469|qid=Q11385469|1=Reisai}} *December 20 – Autumn Festival ({{ill|lt=Niinamesai|Q11501518|qid=Q11501518|1=Niiname-no-Matsuri}}) === History === === Founding === The founding date is unknown, but: *The “Meiji Shrine Records” state: “The founding date is unclear; according to tradition, it was during the {{ill|lt=Wadō|Q1373026|qid=Q1373026|1=Wadō (era)}} era of {{ill|lt=Empress Genmei|Q233224|qid=Q233224|1=Empress Genmei}} (708–715).” *The “Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Photo Register” lists it as “founding unknown.” *The “Newly Compiled Gazetteer of Scenic Sites” states, “It was probably around the Wadō period.” *An information board in the precinct titled “Ami Shrine Grove (Takaku)” (Ami Town Board of Education) gives “Year 15 of {{ill|lt=Empress Suiko|Q298057|qid=Q298057|1=Empress Suiko}} (607).” === Description in the Hitachi Fudoki === Takaku is an area name descended from “Takaki no Sato” (Village of Takaki) mentioned in the {{ill|lt=Hitachi Fudoki|Q11481687|qid=Q11481687|1=Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki}} under the entry for {{ill|lt=Shida District|Q11386034|qid=Q11386034|1=Shida district}}. The outline of the ancient account (the Futsu myth) associated with Takaki no Sato is as follows: :At the dawn of heaven and earth, when plants and trees could speak, a deity named Futsu Ōkami descended from heaven. :The great deity traveled through {{ill|lt=Ashihara no Nakatsukuni|Q1096244|qid=Q1096244|lt=the Central Land of Reed Plains|1=Ashihara no Nakatsukuni}} and pacified the unruly deities of mountains and rivers. :Having completed the act of pacification, the deity intended to return to heaven, immediately removed the staff, armor, spear, shield, sword, and jeweled scepter he wore, left them in this place, and rode a white cloud back up to the blue heavens. :The remainder is omitted. According to this account, Takaki is the sacred site of Futsu Ōkami’s ascent to heaven. The Meiji Shrine Records describe it as “a sacred land of the age of the gods.” In {{ill|lt=classical Japanese|Q11411592|qid=Q11411592|1=classical Japanese}}, the word “ku (来)” carries the meaning “to go,” and the Nihon Kokuyūshi Kō states that “Takaki” means “high going” ({{ill|lt=Great Dictionary of Japanese Place Names|Q17213149|qid=Q17213149|1=Dai Nippon Chimei Jisho}}). The Newly Compiled Gazetteer of Hitachi Province says it derives from the meaning “descending from the High Plain of Heaven,” while adding the note, “However, there may be another meaning.” The Gun-gōkō notes that there is a place in the village called “Tate-nuki-yama,” suggesting that the name may derive from Futsu Ōkami removing his shield. This “Tate-nuki-yama” is said to be the hill behind the shrine buildings. The same place name also exists at the site of {{ill|lt=Tatenui Shrine (Gōnaka, Miho Village)|Q17211507|qid=Q17211507|lt=Tatenui Shrine|1=Tatenui Shrine (Miho, Ibaraki)}}. The Hitachi Fudoki does not explicitly mention the existence of a shrine related to the Futsu myth, but it may have been omitted due to the phrase “the remainder is omitted.” There has been little dispute, from the time of the Newly Compiled Gazetteer of Hitachi Province to the present, regarding identifying Takaku as the descendant place name of “Takaki no Sato”. However, the Great Dictionary of Japanese Place Names proposes its own theory rejecting this common view, and in that context identifies the Shikinaisha Ami Shrine with Nakagō Ami Shrine instead. === Futsu Ōkami === Futsu Ōkami is sometimes considered the same deity as Futsunushi-no-Kami due to the similarity in pronunciation. *The Newly Compiled Gazetteer of Hitachi Province states that Futsu Ōkami may refer to either Futsunushi Ōkami or the deity Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto, but it is unclear which, noting that in the {{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|qid=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}, Takemikazuchi-no-Otoko-no-Kami is also called Takefutsu-no-Kami and Toyofutsu-no-Kami, whereas in the {{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihongi|1=Nihon Shoki}}, Futsunushi-no-Kami and Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto are treated as separate deities, making it difficult to determine which is meant. *The Hyōchū Kofudoki annotates “Futsu Ōkami” as Futsunushi-no-Kami and suggests that both of the two shrines in Shida District listed in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō were likely enshrined based on the Futsu myth. *The Great Dictionary of Japanese Place Names states that, according to the Fudoki, Takaki-no-Kami is Futsu Ōkami, that is, another name for {{ill|lt=Katori Grand Shrine|Q372380|qid=Q372380|lt=Katori Ōkami|1=Katori Jingū}}, though in fact there is no clear textual evidence identifying Futsu Ōkami as another name for Katori Ōkami, and this is thought to be an inference based on phonetic similarity. *Tatenui Shrine refers to its enshrined deity on its precinct signboard as “Futsunushi Ōkami” or “Futsunushi-no-Mikoto”. All sources agree that it is a shrine that enshrines Futsu Ōkami. The Newly Compiled Gazetteer of Hitachi Province, Shrine Records, and the Inashiki District Local History simply state that it “enshrines Futsu Ōkami.” As for the deity’s identity, it is variously identified as Futsunushi-no-Kami or Takemikazuchi-no-Kami (Takefutsu-no-Kami), and there is also an alternative theory, departing from phonetic similarity, that identifies it as {{ill|lt=Toyoki-irihiko-no-Mikoto|Q11633375|qid=Q11633375|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko}}. However, there are several circumstantial indications that this shrine has long been a Kashima shrine enshrining Take-Mikazuchi-no-Otoko-no-Mikoto. *An ancient ritual in which Ami Shrine receives a ritual procession ({{ill|lt=imperial visit|Q11626055|qid=Q11626055|1=Imperial visits}}) of the enshrined deity from Tatenui Shrine was called the “Kashima Ritual” at Tatenui Shrine. *A branch shrine founded in {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|qid=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} 2 (1395) in Nakamura Nishine, {{ill|lt=Tsuchiura City|Q653446|qid=Q653446|1=Tsuchiura}}, enshrines Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto as a Kashima Shrine. Including this shrine, there were several shrines in and around Inashiki District known as “Ninomiya Kashima Shrine.” *In Kakema, Ami Town, there is Kashima Konago Shrine, said to have been founded during the {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|qid=Q1373191|lt=Daidō|1=Daidō}} era (806–810), which enshrines Kashima no Miko-no-Kami. Kashima no Miko-no-Kami is considered a descendant of Takaku Ami Shrine and is enshrined facing west-northwest toward this shrine. *Within the grouping of the “Takaku Three Shrines,” this shrine occupied a position corresponding to {{ill|lt=Kashima Grand Shrine|Q706499|qid=Q706499|1=Kashima Jingū}}, which enshrines Takemikazuchi Ōkami. However, there is also an alternative interpretation in the Gun-gōkō regarding this point. According to the shrine tradition of Murozaki Shrine, in {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|qid=Q1075635|1=Jōgan}} 4 (864) or {{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|qid=Q1346317|1=Ninna}} 3 (887), Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto, one of the three deities enshrined together at Takaku Ami Shrine, was divided and enshrined at Ōmuro following a {{ill|lt=divine oracle|Q217123|qid=Q217123|1=Oracle}}. The Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Photo Register also states that the division of Futsunushi-no-Kami to Toake Shrine in Hasamado occurred at the same time. This division of deities by divine oracle can be seen as a shift from Futsu worship to Kashima worship. === Takaku Three Shrines === The Gun-gōkō records under the entry for “Takaki Shrine” that “with three deities enshrined together, it is called the Takaku Three Shrines,” indicating that the name arose from enshrining three deities. *The Newly Compiled Gazetteer of Hitachi Province and the Meiji Shrine Records do not mention this name. *The Great Dictionary of Japanese Place Names mentions it, citing the Gun-gōkō. *The Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Photo Register states: “The two deities enshrined together were each, in {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|qid=Q1075635|1=Jōgan}} 2 (or, according to another account, {{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|qid=Q1346317|1=Ninna}} 3), possessed by individuals and enshrined in the villages of Hasamado and Ōmuro. Together with this shrine, these are called the Takaku Three Shrines.” This description differs from that of the Gun-gōkō, which treats “Takaku Three Shrines” as another name for Takaki Shrine, instead interpreting it as a collective term for the three shrines including Ōmuro Shrine and Hasamado Shrine. The Gun-gōkō also notes that when an uncultivated area called “Kaminari” in the village was cleared, foundation stones of a shrine site were found, and cultivation was abandoned, suggesting that after this, Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto and Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto were added as associated deities, based on the name “Kaminari (Thunder).” This suggests that in addition to Takaki Shrine, there were two other shrines, and that combining them into a joint enshrinement of three deities naturally gave rise to the name “Takaku Three Shrines.” The collective concept of the Takaku Three Shrines resembles the “Kashima no Ōkami” (the shrines of Ameno-Ōkami, Sakado Shrine, and Numaō Shrine) mentioned in the entry for {{ill|lt=Kashima District (Ibaraki Prefecture)|Q6374307|qid=Q6374307|1=Kashima District, Ibaraki}} in the Hitachi Fudoki. At least after the division by divine oracle, this shrine occupied a position corresponding to “Ameno-Ōkami Shrine” (Kashima Grand Shrine). When connecting this shrine with Murozaki Shrine, the extension leads to the plateau of the Hasamado Castle site, reaching Toake Shrine (which survives today as an independent shrine after being merged into Nakagō Ami Shrine in the Meiji period). These three shrines are arranged in an orderly manner, even in the orientation of their buildings. By the time of the Engishiki Jinmyōchō, the concept of “Kashima no Ōkami” had receded, and from the medieval period onward, the grouping of the {{ill|lt=Three Eastern Shrines|Q17222093|qid=Q17222093|1=Tōgoku Sansha}} (Kashima Grand Shrine, Katori Grand Shrine, {{ill|lt=Ikisu Shrine|Q11073095|qid=Q11073095|1=Ikisu Shrine}}) became dominant. Shrines that enshrine the ancient three deities of “Kashima no Ōkami” as Kashima deities (rather than as {{ill|lt=Kasuga deities|Q11088805|qid=Q11088805|1=Kasuga-no-Kami}}) are rare in Ibaraki Prefecture, remaining only at Takaku Ami Shrine and a few others, such as Kashiwada Shrine (Kashiwada-chō, {{ill|lt=Ushiku City|Q844133|qid=Q844133|1=Ushiku, Ibaraki}}), Takahama Shrine (Takahama, {{ill|lt=Ishioka City|Q819658|qid=Q819658|1=Ishioka, Ibaraki}}), and Momiyama Shrine (Momiyama, {{ill|lt=Hokota City|Q857297|qid=Q857297|1=Hokota, Ibaraki}}). === Relationship with Mimura Takaku Shrine === In the earliest record of the Hitachi Sōsha documents, the “Construction Order Draft for {{ill|lt=Hitachi Province Sōsha Shrine|Q11481689|qid=Q11481689|1=Hitachi-no-Kuni Sōshagū}}” dated the 5th month of {{ill|lt=Jishō|Q1194671|qid=Q1194671|1=Jishō}} 3 (1179), the name “Mimura Takaku Shrine” appears as the party responsible for constructing one hearth hall (three bays). “Mimura” is generally identified with the ancient name of the area around {{ill|lt=Oda (Tsukuba City)|Q28686015|qid=Q28686015|lt=Oda|1=Oda}}, {{ill|lt=Tsukuba City|Q273863|qid=Q273863|1=Tsukuba}}, corresponding to the Three-Village Township of {{ill|lt=Tsukuba District|Q7850092|qid=Q7850092|1=Tsukuba District, Ibaraki}} in ancient times. In another Hitachi Sōsha document, a petition by {{ill|lt=Oda Sadamune|Q11463150|qid=Q11463150|1=Oda clan}} dated the 4th day of the 5th month of {{ill|lt=Bunpō|Q987788|qid=Q987788|1=Bunpō}} 2 (1318) states, “For the Tsukuba Shrine, Mimura Township allotment, there is absolutely no precedent of construction,” and the name “Mimura Takaku Shrine” disappears. The shrines listed in the initial construction order—“{{ill|lt=Mount Tsukuba Shrine|Q11602876|qid=Q11602876|lt=Tsukuba Shrine|1=Tsukubasan Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Yoshida Shrine (Mito City)|Q11414032|qid=Q11414032|lt=Yoshida Shrine|1=Yoshida Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Sato Shrine|Q55541642|qid=Q55541642|1=Sato Shrine (Hitachiota)}}, {{ill|lt=Shizu Shrine|Q11663648|qid=Q11663648|1=Shizu Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Inada Shrine|Q11596799|qid=Q11596799|1=Inada Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Ōkunitama Shrine (Sakuragawa City)|Q11433665|qid=Q11433665|1=Okunitama Shrine}}”—refer to main shrines rather than branch shrines, so “Mimura Takaku Shrine” is treated as a major shrine listed alongside representative Shikinaisha of Hitachi Province. However, since the name presupposes the existence of a separate main Takaku Shrine, this shrine alone is thought to have been a branch shrine. No successor shrine exists today for this major shrine, nor are there nearby place names corresponding to “Takaku.” In the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|qid=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}, Mimura Township had a prominent temple called Mimurayama Seireiin Gokuraku-ji, but today only scant traces remain. It is possible that “Mimura Takaku Shrine” was part of this temple complex. The Hitachi Sōsha documents indicate that at least during that period there existed a grouping called “Takaku Shrine” or “Takaku Deity.” This can also be interpreted as documentary evidence that a “Takaku Shrine” (rather than Ami Shrine) existed under a name older than “Ninomiya Myōjin.” === Early modern period and later === In the early modern period, it was called “Ninomiya Myōjin” as a shrine second in rank only to Tatenui Shrine within Shida District, and became the general shrine for 45 villages in the western half of the district. A petition dated {{ill|lt=Eiwa (Japan)|Q1145780|qid=Q1145780|1=Eiwa}} 1 (1375) already states, “Above all, the two shrines of Kihara and Takaku are the foremost general shrines within the estate” (Hyōchū Kofudoki). These two shrines share parallel histories as sacred sites of the Futsu myth, as candidate shrines for the two Shikinaisha of Shida District listed in the Engishiki Jinmyōchō, as the first and second shrines of the district, and as former prefectural shrines under the modern ranking system. A ridge tag from {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|qid=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 4 (1691) marking the construction of the main sanctuary still exists. The three-bay nagare-zukuri main sanctuary is the oldest building in Ami Town. In {{ill|lt=Hōreki|Q1194397|qid=Q1194397|1=Hōreki}} 6 (1756), a shrine temple later converted into the kagura hall was built. During the Edo period, disputes arose among Nakagō Ami Shrine, Tachinokoshi Kumano Gongen (later merged into Nakagō Ami Shrine), and the Shikinaisha Ami Shrine regarding identification. The Meiji Shrine Records state that while sources such as the shrine’s own register, Nijūhassha-kō, Jingi Shiryō, and Tokusen Jinmyōchō identify this shrine as the Shikinaisha Ami Shrine of Shida District, other works such as Gun-gōkō and Shikisha-kō differ, with Shikisha-kō and the Place Name Dictionary identifying the Ami Shrine of Ami Village as the Shikinaisha, leaving the matter for future consideration. Takaku and Ami are both ancient place names derived from Takaki Township and Ami Township in the {{ill|lt=Wamyō Ruijushō|Q2702542|qid=Q2702542|1=Wamyō Ruijushō}}, and even under the Meiji {{ill|lt=Municipal System|Q11578244|qid=Q11578244|1=Municipal System}}, they were separated as Takaku Village (later {{ill|lt=Funashima Village|Q11613763|qid=Q11613763|1=Funashima}}) and Ami Village, indicating distinct cultural regions. In October {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 (1873), together with Tatenui Shrine, the first shrine of Shida District, it was ranked as a prefectural shrine under the modern system and became the tutelary shrine for eight villages centered on Takaku. At this time, the shrine was renamed Ami Shrine. The shrine name stone bears the inscription “Suspended Shrine, Engishiki-listed Second Shrine, Ami Shrine,” and the worship hall plaque donated by the {{ill|lt=chief priest|Q1145048|qid=Q1145048|lt=Ōmiya-shi|1=Kyōhō}} of {{ill|lt=Kashima Grand Shrine|Q706499|qid=Q706499|1=Kashima Jingū}} reads “Prefectural Shrine Ami Shrine.” There were only 16 former prefectural shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture (17 including government-designated Gokoku shrines), and among minor Shikinaisha of Hitachi Province, only the two shrines of Shida District attained this rank. Compared with other former prefectural shrines, these two are little known and have almost no value as {{ill|lt=tourist destinations|Q570116|qid=Q570116|1=Tourist attraction}}. Nevertheless, with their stately groves and shrine buildings befitting their history, they have preserved a tranquil atmosphere to the present day. In {{ill|lt=Shōwa|Q327567|qid=Q327567|1=Shōwa era}} 52 (1977), the grove was designated a natural monument by Ami Town. In Shōwa 57 (1982), the shrine grounds were designated an Ibaraki Prefecture Green Environment Conservation Area (No. 20, Ami). A giant tree stands behind the main sanctuary. While the grove is well preserved, the bamboo forest behind it is sometimes neglected. The shrine grounds lie on a {{ill|lt=tongue-shaped plateau|Q97216015|qid=Q97216015|1=tongue-shaped plateau}} facing Kasumigaura, and all the paths extending from around the shrine buildings descend steeply. The innermost area behind the shrine (aza Hana-no-I) is the site of the medieval Takaku Manor, and to the west is the Middle Jōmon-period Neda Shell Mound (Takaku Shell Mound). The area including Takaku Junior High School to the south contains the Takaku archaeological site. === Related shrines === ;Murozaki Shrine :Ibaraki Prefecture, Inashiki District, Ami Town, Akebono (formerly Ōmuro 494, Shida District) :Enshrined deity: Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto :*In {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|qid=Q1075635|1=Jōgan}} 4 (864) or {{ill|lt=Ninna|Q1346317|qid=Q1346317|1=Ninna}} 3 (887), following a divine oracle, Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto, one of the deities enshrined together at Takaku Ami Shrine, was divided and enshrined here. Formerly an unranked shrine under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. :*The “Akebono {{ill|lt=gumi (plant)|Q161042|qid=Q161042|lt=silverberry|1=Elaeagnus}},” designated a natural monument by Ibaraki Prefecture, once stood at the horse grounds of Murozaki Shrine before land readjustment. ;Toake Shrine :Ibaraki Prefecture, Inashiki District, Ami Town, Hasamado (formerly Hasamado Village, Shida District) :Enshrined deity: Futsunushi-no-Kami :*History unknown. According to the Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Photo Register, like Murozaki Shrine, Futsunushi-no-Kami was divided and enshrined here following a divine oracle in the same year. Formerly an unranked shrine. :*In April {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 41 (1908), it was merged into Nakagō Ami Shrine, but continues to exist as an {{ill|lt=independent shrine|Q7882536|qid=Q7882536|1=Non-denominational}}. :*The tongue-shaped plateau on which the shrine grounds and the Mahoroba Welfare Center stand is the site of the medieval Hasamado Castle, and its slopes contain the Hasamado Shell Mound from the middle to late {{ill|lt=Jōmon period|Q52824|qid=Q52824|1=Jōmon period}}. From the shrine grounds, one can see not only nearby Kasumigaura but also {{ill|lt=Mount Tsukuba|Q1754619|qid=Q1754619|1=Mount Tsukuba}} in the distance. At the foot of the terrace is the {{ill|lt=Yokaren Peace Memorial Museum|Q122852747|qid=Q122852747|1=Yokaren Peace Memorial Museum}}. ;Kashima Konago Shrine :Ibaraki Prefecture, Inashiki District, Ami Town, Kakema 543 (formerly Kakema Village, Shida District) :Enshrined deity: Kashima no Miko-no-Kami :Associated deities: Takemikazuchi-no-Otoko-no-Mikoto, Futsunushi-no-Mikoto, Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto, {{ill|qid=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|qid=Q3080728|qid=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Mikoto|1=Ukanomitama}}, {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|UNKNOWN|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|lt=Amaterasu|Q455602|qid=Q455602|lt=Ōhirume-no-Muchi|1=Amaterasu}} :*Said to have been founded during the {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|qid=Q1373191|lt=Daidō|1=Daidō}} era (806–810). Former name: Konago Shrine. Former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|qid=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. :*Enshrined facing west-northwest toward Takaku Ami Shrine. ;Kashima Shrine :Ibaraki Prefecture, Tsuchiura City, Nakamura Nishine 817-1 (formerly Nakamura Nishine Village, Shida District) :Enshrined deity: Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto :*Said to have been founded in {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|qid=Q1193078|1=Ōei}} 2 (1395) as a division from Takaku Ami Shrine. Former name: Kashima Ninomiya Myōjin (Newly Compiled Gazetteer of Hitachi Province). Former village shrine. :*Main sanctuary (three-bay nagare-zukuri) built in {{ill|lt=Kyōhō|Q1145048|qid=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 8 (1723); worship hall built in {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|qid=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}} 5 (1822) (designated cultural property of Tsuchiura City). === Notes === {{Help:Footnotes}} {{Reflist|2|refs= The original text in the “Hitachi Fudoki” reads: “'''Takaki no Sato'''. The elders say: At the dawn of heaven and earth, when plants and trees could speak, a deity descended from heaven named '''Futsu Ōkami'''. He traveled through Ashihara no Nakatsukuni and pacified the unruly kinds of mountains and rivers. When the great deity had completed the act of pacification, he intended to return to heaven. Immediately, he removed the staff he carried (commonly called Ikawa no Kawaye), armor, spear, shield, sword, and the jeweled scepter he held, leaving them all in this place. Then he rode a white cloud and ascended back to the blue heavens. The remainder is omitted.” The outline is based on the annotated reading in the Hyōchū Kofudoki. Regarding “Kashima no Ōkami,” the Hitachi Fudoki contains the following passages. The outline is based on the annotated reading in the Hyōchū Kofudoki. *It states: “In that place are the shrines of Ameno-Ōkami, Sakado Shrine, and Numaō Shrine, collectively called Kashima no Ōkami. Hence the district was named thus (according to popular tradition, the land of Kasumigaura is Kashima).” From the fact that three shrines called “Kashima no Ōkami” were enshrined, the name “Kashima District” arose. “That place” refers to the “divine district” established by allocating land from the southern part of Karino of Umi-no-kuni and the northern part of Samuta of Naka-no-kuni at the request of Ōotonoue Nakatomi no Kamako and Ōotonoshita Nakatomi no Usako. *Another passage states: “Before clarity and turbidity were distinguished, before heaven and earth and the flavors of plants, the ancestral heavenly gods (commonly called Kagamiro Mikaro-gi) assembled the eight million gods at the High Plain of Heaven. Then the ancestral heavenly gods declared: ‘Now our imperial grandchild will rule and pacify the land of abundant reed plains and fertile rice fields. From the High Plain of Heaven, a great deity will descend, named Kashima Ameno-Ōkami.’ Thus heaven named it Kashima Palace, and earth named it Toyokashima Palace.” (This is followed by passages concerning the construction of the divine palace.) From this, it is understood that the origin of the collective name “Kashima no Ōkami” lies in the divine name “Kashima Ameno-Ōkami.” Hana-no-I (Hana-i) was a minor place name in the former Takaku Village (Newly Compiled Gazetteer of Hitachi Province, village section). This sub-area name remains only in the innermost part of the shrine grounds. The Great Dictionary of Japanese Place Names argues that Takaki no Sato in the Hitachi Fudoki and Takaki Township in the Wamyō Ruijushō originally referred to different areas, identifying ancient Takaki no Sato with the former Asahi Village (southern Ami Town). It further claims that since Futsu Ōkami, originally enshrined at Tatenui Shrine, was later divided and enshrined at present-day Takaku Ami Shrine, the ranking of first and second shrines arose. However, the ranking of first and second shrines has no universal validity as evidence of main-branch shrine relationships, nor can it explain the fact that the two shrines conducted the “Kashima Ritual.” Moreover, within the entire former Shida District, the only place name with the sound “takaku” is Takaku itself. {{Cite journal|Japanese|author=Toyosaki Taku |date=1962-12 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10109/10178 |title=An Inquiry into the Shida District Government Office |journal=Bulletin of the College of Arts and Sciences, Ibaraki University. Humanities |issue=13 |pages=17–32 |hdl=10109/10178 |id={{CRID|1570009752731971072}}}} There is also an alternative theory identifying “Tatenui-no-Kami” as {{ill|lt=Hikosashiri-no-Mikoto|Q135327097|qid=Q135327097|1=Hikosachi no Mikoto}}. See also {{ill|lt=Tatenui Shrine (Gōnaka, Miho Village)|Q17211507|qid=Q17211507|lt=Tatenui Shrine|1=Tatenui Shrine (Miho, Ibaraki)}} in Miho Village. The content of the Hitachi Sōsha documents is based on the “Fuchū Sōsha Documents” included in the Newly Compiled Gazetteer of Hitachi Province. Hida Hideyo, “[http://www.rekishikan.museum.ibk.ed.jp/10_kanndayori/dayori97.htm Material Introduction: Roof Tiles Excavated from the Ruins of Mimurayama Seireiin Gokuraku-ji]” {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefectural Museum of History|Q4676321|qid=Q4676321|1=Ibaraki Prefectural Museum of History}}, History Museum Bulletin No. 97, July 2007. Accessed March 2020. Ami Town, “One Hundred Scenic Sites of Ami Town.” [http://inasikinokamigami.jp/ Gods of Inashiki] (Inashiki Branch, Ibaraki Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines): [http://inasikinokamigami.jp/jinja/4.ami.html Shrines of Ami Town]. Accessed November 5, 2013. Link broken as of March 2020 ([https://web.archive.org/web/20160304215531/http://inasikinokamigami.jp/jinja/4.ami.html Archive] via the {{ill|lt=Wayback Machine|Q648266|qid=Q648266|lt=Internet Archive Wayback Machine|1=Wayback Machine}}). Regarding the founding of Murozaki Shrine, the Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Photo Register gives Jōgan 2, while the Inashiki Branch of the Ibaraki Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines gives Jōgan 4. There is no discrepancy regarding the Ninna date. }} === References === *Documents available via the [https://www.ndl.go.jp/ National Diet Library] and the [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ National Diet Library Digital Collection]. :Nakayama Nobuna and Kurita Hiroshi (eds.), “Newly Compiled Gazetteer of Hitachi Province, Vol. 2.” Sekizenkansha. Meiji 32–34 (1899–1901). :Yoshida Tōgo, “{{ill|lt=Great Dictionary of Japanese Place Names|Q17213149|qid=Q17213149|1=Dai Nippon Chimei Jisho}},” Vol. 2, 2nd ed. Fuzanbō. October 17, Meiji 40 (1907). :Meiji Shrine Records Compilation Office (ed.), “Meiji Shrine Records: Prefectural and Local Shrines (Upper).” Meiji Shrine Records Compilation Office. Meiji 45 (1912). :Ibaraki Shimbun, “Ibaraki Prefecture Shrine Photo Register.” Ibaraki Shimbunsha. Shōwa 16 (1942). :Kurita Hiroshi, annotated by Fujikura Shirō, “Annotated Ancient Fudoki: Hitachi.” Ōokayama Shoten. Shōwa 5 (1930). :Kurita Hiroshi, “Shrine Records.” Meiji 9–20 (1876–1887). Onkodo. :Hanawa Izumine (ed.), “Inashiki District Local History with Honor Roll.” Shūkyō Shimbunsha. Taishō 15 (1926). *Miyamoto Mototama, “Hitachi Records: Gun-gōkō (Hitachi Province Gun-gōkō).” Man’en 1 (1860). Available via the National Institute of Japanese Literature. Accessed March 2020. *[http://www.town.ami.lg.jp/ Ami Town], “[http://www.town.ami.lg.jp/category/1-9-3-0-0.html History and Culture (One Hundred Scenic Sites of Ami Town)].” March, Heisei 9 (1997). Accessed March 2020. {{Shinto Navigation}}{{List of Shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture Navigation}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ami Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ami Shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:阿見町の歴史]] [[Category:阿見町の建築物]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]]
